coredata.dc:title: Secondary toxic effect of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots alters the expression of miR-21 and miR-29a in human cell lines
coredata.dc:creator: Hashemi, Mahnaz Sadat
Gharbi, Sedigheh
Jafarinejad-Farsangi, Saeideh
Ansari-Asl, Zeinab
Dezfuli, Amin Shiralizadeh
coredata.dc:description: For in vitro studies, non-toxic doses of nanomaterials are routinely selected by quantification of live cells after exposing to different concentrations of nanoparticles but considering only morphological changes or viability of cells is not sufficient to conclude that these nanomaterials are non-cytotoxic. Here we investigated if secondary toxicity is active in the cells exposed to non-toxic doses of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Non-cytotoxic dose of 15 μg mL−1 of GO (100 nm) and GQDs (50 nm) was selected according to MTT and Hoechst 33342/PI double staining assays. In order to investigate the secondary toxicity, the expression of miR-21, miR-29a and three genes at both mRNA and protein level were evaluated in MCF-7, HUVEC, KMBC/71 cells 4 and 24 h post exposure. Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was assessed by Rhodamine 123 staining. According to our results, there was no significant decrease in viability of cells after exposure to the non-cytotoxic dose of GO and GQDs, but we observed significant alterations in the expression level of miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes after treatment in all three cells. In addition to molecular changes, we observed alteration in mitochondrial activity at cellular level. However, we also observed that GO influenced the basal level of genes and MMP more compare to GQDs. Considering that all these genes are involved in breast tumor development and metastasis, the observed changes in miRNA expression and protein synthesis may alter cell fate and susceptibility and cause deviation in the desired outcome of GO and GQDs application in medical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
MicroRNA-21
MicroRNA-29a
MCF-7 cells
HUVEC cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of cell apoptosis by Hoechst 33342/PI double staining
Quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR)
Protein extraction and Western blot analysis
Cytotoxicity of GO and GQDs on MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells
Apoptosis of MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells using Hoechst 33342/PI double staining
miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN RNA expression in response to non-cytotoxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50
Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN protein expression in response to non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50
Effect of non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 on mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of GO and GQDs nanographenes (A) FTIR analysis (B) UV–visible and fluorescence spectra. TEM images for GQDs (C) and large-sized GO sheets (D).
The effect of GO-100 and GQDs-50 on viability of A) MCF-7, B) KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells after 4 and 24 h. * P < .01, *** P < .001 and **** p < .0001.
Fluorescence microscopy images of A) MCF-7, B) KMBC/71 and C) HUVEC cells treated with the non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 (15 μg mL−1) and stained with Hoechst33342 and PI.

Relative expression of miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN in MCF-7, KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells. These cells were exposed to GO-100 and GQDs-50 at concentration of 15 μg mL−1 for 4 and 24 h. * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P < .001, **** P < .0001.

A) Experimental design of the study. B) The effects of non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 (15 μg mL−1) on the protein level of Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes in MCF-7, KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells. * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P < .001.

The effects of non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 (15 μg mL−1) on mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP). Cells were stained with Rhodamine 123. Fluorescent microscopy images of A) MCF-7, B) KMBC/71 and C) HUVEC cells. D) Fluorescent intensity was determined using Microplate Fluorescence Reader. ** P < .01, **** P < .0001.
The percentage of viability of the cells at concentration 15 μg mL−1 of GO-100 and GQD-50.
List of primer sequences used for cDNA synthesis and qRT-PCR in this study.

head.ce:title: Secondary toxic effect of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots alters the expression of miR-21 and miR-29a in human cell lines

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Ebne Sina Avenue, Jahad Blvd, Kerman 76198-13159, Iran; Department of Chemistry, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz 61357-43311, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ebne Sina Avenue, Jahad Blvd, Kerman 76198-13159, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For in vitro studies, non-toxic doses of nanomaterials are routinely selected by quantification of live cells after exposing to different concentrations of nanoparticles but considering only morphological changes or viability of cells is not sufficient to conclude that these nanomaterials are non-cytotoxic. Here we investigated if secondary toxicity is active in the cells exposed to non-toxic doses of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Non-cytotoxic dose of 15 μg mL−1 of GO (100 nm) and GQDs (50 nm) was selected according to MTT and Hoechst 33342/PI double staining assays. In order to investigate the secondary toxicity, the expression of miR-21, miR-29a and three genes at both mRNA and protein level were evaluated in MCF-7, HUVEC, KMBC/71 cells 4 and 24 h post exposure. Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was assessed by Rhodamine 123 staining. According to our results, there was no significant decrease in viability of cells after exposure to the non-cytotoxic dose of GO and GQDs, but we observed significant alterations in the expression level of miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes after treatment in all three cells. In addition to molecular changes, we observed alteration in mitochondrial activity at cellular level. However, we also observed that GO influenced the basal level of genes and MMP more compare to GQDs. Considering that all these genes are involved in breast tumor development and metastasis, the observed changes in miRNA expression and protein synthesis may alter cell fate and susceptibility and cause deviation in the desired outcome of GO and GQDs application in medical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well-known members of carbon-based materials with two-dimensional structure of hexagonal arrangement of carbon atoms (Dasari Shareena et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2016; Tabish et al., 2018). They have attracted many attention to be used as suitable carrier of anticancer agents owing to their unique properties such as high surface-to-volume ratio, having oxygen-containing active groups on their surface, high capacity of drug loading and high solubility (Liu et al., 2017; Some et al., 2014; Gurunathan et al., 2019). GO is an oxidized form of graphene which can be converted to GQDs (Liu et al., 2017; Gurunathan et al., 2019). GQDs are a new form of nanographenes which can be used as biocompatible imaging probe in simultaneous tracing and drug delivery due to their small size and auto-fluorescence capability (Tang et al., 2016; Qiu et al., 2015a; Elvati et al., 2017). Using these nanographenes, the challenges of drug delivery including low solubility, rapid excretion from body, low biocompatibility and low targeting can be overcome (Yuan et al., 2014; McCallion et al., 2016; Navya et al., 2019). More importantly, confirming the biocompatibility of these nanographenes is a critical factor in the medical research (Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2013). It is notable that nanoparticles can affect cells mostly through primary and secondary toxicity. While primary toxicity includes visible damages after exposure of cells to nanoparticles, the second toxicity changes the primed state of the cell which may cause adverse outcome.

For in vitro studies, biosafety level of nanoparticles in different cell types is routinely assessed by MTT, WST8/1, CCK8 and LDH methods which define the rate of cell death or viability at different doses of nanoparticle (Ryoo et al., 2010; Gies and Zou, 2017) which is a kind of primary toxicity assessment. What is important is that if the safe dose of GO and GQDs have secondary toxicity effect on the primed state of gene expression of cells or not. The changes in the expression state of genes may be in the opposite direction of the desired outcome of drug delivery.

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are non-coding single-strand RNAs of 21 to 25 nucleotides that regulate the expression of genes at post-transcriptional levels. MiR-21 and miR-29a are well known miRNAs in tumorigenesis in various types of cancers including breast cancer. Reduced expression of these two miRNAs have been suggested as therapeutic strategies to induce apoptosis and eliminate tumor cells (Badr, 2016; Pei et al., 2016; Khamisipour et al., 2018). Considering that GO and GQDs have great potential as nanocarriers, our objective is to investigate if non-toxic dose of GO (100 nm) and GQD (50 nm) which does not induce significant cell death affect the primed state of the expression of miR-21, miR-29a Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes in MCF-7 (breast cancer cell line), KMBC/71 (Kerman Medical Breast Cancer 71) and HUVEC (Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cell) cells.

GQDs were synthesized through carbonization of citric acid (CA) based on the reported procedure (Dong et al., 2012). 5 g CA was heated in an oil bath at 200 °C. The formation of GQDs can be observed by color changing from colorless to yellow of the liquefied CA. In order to neutralize the media, 0.1 M NaOH solution was added to the obtained GQDs dropwise under vigorous stirring. Then, the solution was put into a dialysis bag (1000 Da) and dialyzed in distilled water for 48 h. During this time, distilled water was replaced twice. After dialysis, the GQDs were dried using a freeze dryer (24 h) and the product was dissolved in EtOH at a concentration of 500 mg mL−1.

GO was prepared as previously reported (Jafari et al., 2018). To prepare the GO solution, 1 mg of graphite oxide was dispersed in 1 mL of distilled water and sonicated at 4 °C for 2 h to obtain the appropriate form of GO. The solution was then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min and the precipitate was separated from the supernatant.

MCF-7, Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) and Kerman medical breast cancer 71 (KMBC/71) cells were cultured in Dulbecco modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and DMEM /F12 supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin (100 mg mL−1) and streptomycin (100 mg mL−1) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 (v/v), respectively. KMBC/71 cells are primary cultured breast tumor cells from a man with breast cancer.

The MTT test was used to assess GO (100 nm) (GO-100) and GQDs (50 nm) (GQDs-50) toxicity. In this test, MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells were seeded on 96-well plates (104 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. After that, cells were treated with different concentrations (7.8, 15.6, 31.2, 62.5, 125 μg mL−1) of GO-100 and GQDs-50 for 4 and 24 h. Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Then, the resultant formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide). The absorbance of each well was measured by a microplate reader at 570 nm. Then, the relative cell viability (%) was calculated as a percentage relative to untreated control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cell apoptosis by Hoechst 33342/PI double staining
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 and HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells were cultured in 96-well plates for 24 h. Afterwards, cells were treated with GO-100 and GQDs-50 at concentration of 15 μg mL−1. After 4 and 24 h, the treatments were removed and fresh growth medium was added. Then, the cells were stained with PBS solution containing Hoechst 33342 (10 μg mL−1) and PI (10 μg mL−1). After 2–3 min, cells were observed under a fluorescence microscopy. Then, to determine the percentage of apoptotic cells, up to 400 target cells were counted and analyzed using fluorescence microscopy images. The percentages of apoptotic cells estimated according to: live cells with apoptotic nuclei + dead cells with apoptotic nuclei / whole cells ×100 (Syed Abdul Rahman et al., 2013).

Total RNA was extracted manually using RiboEx Total RNA isolation solution (Gene ALL, Korea). Complementary DNA (cDNA) synthesis was performed from total RNA using PrimeScript™ RT Reagent kit (Takara, Japan) according the manufacturer's instruction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR)
ce:section.ce:para: The expression level of miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes were determined using RealQ Plus 2× Master Mix Amplicon Green according to the manufacturer's protocol. RNU6 and 18S rRNA were used as internal control for normalization of miRNAs and other genes expression, respectively. The primer sequences of miR-21 (Li et al., 2011a), miR-29a (Qiu et al., 2015b), Bax (Cuellar et al., 2016), Bcl2 (Zhao et al., 2018) and PTEN (Chen et al., 2018) were listed in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein extraction and Western blot analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Total protein was extracted from cultured cells using RIPA buffer. Protein concentration and purity were measured using Bradford assay. Western blot analysis was performed as previously described with some modifications (Jabarpour et al., 2018; Jafarinejad-Farsangi et al., 2019). Proteins were denatured with equal volume of 5× Laemmli sample buffer and boiled for 5 min. Then proteins were separated according to their molecular weight on SDS-PAGE gels and subsequently transferred to a 0.2 μm immune-Blot™ polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Cat No: 162-017777; Bio-Rad Laboratories, CA, USA). The membranes were then blocked with 5% BSA (Cat No: A-7888; Sigma Aldrich, MO, USA) in 0.1% Tween 20 for 1 h. Then, the membranes were incubated with anti-Bax (Cat No: ab32503, abcam), anti-Bcl2 (Cat No: ab32124, abcam), anti-PTEN (Cat No: ab32199, abcam) and anti-beta actin antibodies (Cat No: ab8227; Abcam) for 1 h at room temperature. Subsequently, membranes were washed three times with TBST and incubated with Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG H&L (HRP) (Cat No: ab6721; Abcam) and Goat anti-mouse IgG (HRP) (Cat No: ab97240; Abcam) secondary antibodies. The membranes were then incubated with enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) for 1–2 min. Protein expression was normalized to β-actin. Densitometry of protein bands was performed using NIH ImageJ software.

The mitochondrial membrane potential of MCF-7, KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells was assessed using the fluorescent dye Rhodamine 123 (Han et al., 2017). In living cells, rhodamine 123 penetrates through the plasma membrane and is sequestered by mitochondria. 5 × 103 cells per well were cultured in a 96-well plate and treated the next day with 15 μg mL−1 GO-100 and GQDs-50 for 4 and 24 h. Following treatment, the culture media was removed and cells were washed twice with PBS. Then staining solution containing 5 μg mL−1 rhodamine 123 in PBS was added for 10 min and incubated at 37 °C in the dark. Cells were observed under an inverted fluorescence microscopy. The fluorescence intensity was measured using Microplate Fluorescence Reader (FLx800, BioTek).
Data were analyzed using the GraphPad Prism software 6.07 (one-way ANOVA). P-value <.05 were considered significant. Fold change of gene expression calculated using 2-∆∆Ct method.
GO and GQDs were characterized by (Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy) FT-IR, ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) techniques.

As shown in the Fig. 1A, GQDs exhibit absorption bands of oxygen-containing functional groups including CO/COOH, OH and C-O-C. The peak about 1363 cm−1 indicates the existence of COO−, while the broad peak at the range of 3000–3600 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching mode of the carboxylic acid group. Furthermore, the observed peaks at 1022, 1203, 1720 and about 1620 cm−1, are assigned to COC stretching, COH stretching, CO stretching and CC stretching mode of vibration, respectively (Dong et al., 2012; Hosseini et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2011). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs is shown in Fig. 1B. Beside a typical absorption peak at 230 nm which is assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, a broad band at 335 nm is observed which is related to n → π* transition of carbonyl groups (Wang et al., 2011; Fuyuno et al., 2014). In addition, GQDs show a strong fluorescent spectrum with a maximum at 435 nm by the excitation at 335 nm. Fig. 1C shows a TEM image of GQDs. Their diameters are mainly distributed in the range of 40–50 nm.

The FT-IR spectrum of GO, Fig. 1A, exhibited a strong peak in the region 1108 cm−1, that corresponded to the CO bond. The peak at 1402 cm−1 corresponds to the C-OH vibrations. Also the peak at 1616 cm−1 and 1714 cm−1 are associated with the CH and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The characteristic absorption peaks at 2852 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1 are related to symmetric and asymmetric –CH2 stretching vibrations, respectively. The UV–Vis spectrum of the GO is given in Fig. 1B. The GO shows a peak around 230 nm that is related to π → π* transition of the CC bonds.
Fig. 1D shows a TEM image of large-sized GO sheets. However, GO sheets with the size of 100 nm -based on dynamic light scattering (DLS) analyze- were used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of GO and GQDs on MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To select the non-toxic dose of GO (100 nm) (GO-100) and GQD (50 nm) (GQD-50), in vitro toxicity against MCF-7, Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) and Kerman medical breast cancer 71 (KMBC/71) cells was determined using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, no significant mortality was observed in MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells exposed to GO-100 and GQDs-50 at different concentrations ranging from 7.8 to 62.5 μg mL−1 after 4 and 24 h. In contrast, HUVEC cells exhibited high viability at 4 h compared to 24 h (Fig. 2C). The percentage of viable cells treated with these compounds at concentration of 15 μg mL−1 summarized in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, viability of MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells was ≥90% at the concentration of 15 μg mL−1 4 and 24 h after exposure to GO-100 and GQDs-50. Viability of HUVEC cells was more than 95% during 4 h after treatment but, it decreased to 85% after 24 h of exposure (GO-100 (P = .05); GQDs-50 (P < .0001) (Table 1). Collectively, our results showed that both agents were non-toxic for MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells 4 and 24 h after treatment, but GO-100 and GQDs-50 induced significant decrease in viability of HUVEC cells 24 h after treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis of MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells using Hoechst 33342/PI double staining
ce:section.ce:para: In the next step, we used Hoechst 33342/PI double staining method to evaluate the occurrence of apoptosis in MCF-7, HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells 4 and 24 h after exposure to GO-100 and GQDs-50.

Double staining with Hoechst 33342/PI classified cells into four groups (Syed Abdul Rahman et al., 2013): live cells with normal nuclei (light blue nuclei with organized chromatin), live cells with apoptotic nuclei (bright blue nuclei with fragmented chromatin), dead cells with normal nuclei (red nuclei with organized chromatin) and dead cells with apoptotic nuclei (red nuclei with fragmented chromatin). According to fluorescent microscopy, morphology of treated MCF-7 (Fig. 3A) and KMBC/71 (Fig. 3B) cells was intact and similar to control cells and the population of apoptotic cells with red nucleus were very few and slightly increased 24 h after exposure. Although apoptosis observed in a very few number of HUVEC cells 4 h after exposure (Fig. 3C), the population of apoptotic cells with morphological changes increased 24 h after exposure. As shown in Table 2 , the percentage of apoptotic cells in MCF-7 cells treated with GO-100 and GQDs-50 was 3.75% and 0.5% at 4 h, which increased to 4.75% and 4.5% at 24 h, respectively. The rate of apoptosis in KMBC/71 cells were less than 5% after exposure to non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 which was not significant. Furthermore, this percentage was 8% and 3% in HUVEC cells 4 h after treatment, which increased to 9.5% and 15% at 24 h, respectively. As a result, HUVEC cells were more sensitive to apoptosis than MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells. According to MTT and Hoechst 33342/PI double staining assay the concentration of 15 μg mL−1 of GO-100 and GQDs-50 was toxic for HUVEC cells after 24 h exposure. Therefore, we investigated the secondary toxicity effect of GO-100 and GQDs-50 on HUVEC cells only after 4 h exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN RNA expression in response to non-cytotoxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the expression of miR-21, miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN which are involved in tumorigenesis, survival and apoptosis of MCF-7, KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells (Fig. 4 ). We observed the fluctuation of miR-21 in MCF-7 (GO-100: 2.87 fold, P = .001; GQDs-50: −1.84 fold, P < .05), KMBC/71 (GO-100: −2 fold, P = .002; GQDs-50: −1.93 fold, P < .05) and HUVEC cells (GO-100: 4 h: 2.43 fold, P = .0010 (Fig. 4).

However, the expression of miR-29 changed only in MCF-7 by GO-100 (4 h: 1.73 fold, P < .05; 24 h: 2 fold, P < .05) and in KMBC/71 cells by GO-100 (−2 fold, P = .003) and GQDs-50 (−2 fold, P = .001). There was no significant change in the expression of miR-29a in HUVEC cells. We observed severe fluctuation in the expression of Bax by GO-100 (30 fold, P > .001) and GQD-50 (36.3 fold, P = .0005) in MCF-7 cells which returned to the normal level after 24 h. In contrast to MCF-7 cells expression of Bax decreased in KMBC/71 (GO-100: −2.48 fold, P < .05) and HUVEC (GO-100: −5 fold, P < .05; GQDs-50: −2 fold, P < .05) cells. The expression of Bcl2 mRNA changed after 24 h of exposure to GQDs-50 (−2.5 fold, P < .05) and 4 h of exposure to GO-100 (−1.7 fold, P < .05) in MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells, respectively. The expression of PTEN mRNA was affected by GO-100 (4 h: −3 fold, P < .0001; 24 h: −26.2 fold, P < .0001) and GQDs-50 (−9 fold: P > .001) in MCF-7 cells and was affected by GO-100 in KMBC/71 (−4 fold, P < .05) and HUVEC (−17.6 fold, P < .05) cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN protein expression in response to non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, to examine whether the significant changes observed in mRNA expression were transient or not, we evaluated the expression of proteins 48 h after treatments (Fig. 5A) (Meng et al., 2020). Our results showed that, the non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 altered the expression of Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN proteins in all tested cells (Fig. 5B). We observed an increase in the Bax protein level in MCF-7 cells after treatment with GO-100 (4 h: 3 fold, P < .01; 24 h: 3.4 fold, P < .01) and GQDs-50 (4 h: 4 fold, P < .01; 24 h: 1.7 fold, P < .05). On the other hand, in HUVEC and KMBC/71 cells only GO-100 induced significant changes (1.8 fold, P < .05; 1.5 fold, P < .05) in the Bax protein level, respectively. In addition to Bax, PTEN protein level was under the influence of both GO-100 (4 h: 1.48 fold, P < .05; 24 h: 5 fold, P < .001) and GQDs-50 (3.2 fold, P < .01) in MCF-7 cells, while only GO-100 influenced PTEN protein in HUVEC (1.36 fold, P < .05) and KMBC/71 (5.78 fold, P < .001) cells. We also observed a significant decrease in the Bcl2 protein expression after treatment with GQDs-50 (0.6 fold, P < .01) and GO-100 (0.2 fold, P < .001) in MCF-7 and KMBC/71, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50 on mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP)
ce:section.ce:para: We observed that MMP was higher in MCF-7 (4 h: 3.4 fold, P = .003; 24 h: 4.4 fold, P = .002) and KMBC/71 (4 h: 2 fold, P = .003; 24 h: 2.6 fold, P < .0001) cells treated with 15 μg mL−1 of GO-100 compare to control groups (Fig. 6 ). The effect of GQDs-50 on MMP was less than GO-100 and induced significant decrease (−2 fold, P < .01) in HUVEC cells after 4 h treatment.

Since, nanographenes have attracted much attention in drug delivery due to their unique characteristics, examination of their toxicity is an important factor which needs to be considered before using them in drug delivery (Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2013). Indeed, nanocarriers for drug delivery should exert the least toxic effect on the target cell and minimum drug interference. Usually, for in vitro studies, cultured cells are exposed to different concentration of nanoparticles to identify the non-toxic dose which does not induce cell death using MTT, WST8/1, CCK8 and LDH methods. But, the question is if a nanomaterial predisposes cells to other stresses at non-toxic doses. Here, we selected the non-toxic dose of 15 μg mL−1 for graphene oxide (GO-100 nm) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs-50 nm) according to MTT assay in two human breast tumor cells (MCF-7 and KMBC/71) and one normal cell line (human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). We observed no significant rate of apoptosis after treatment with the non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQDs-50. Then, we investigated if GO-100 and GQDs-50 could change the basal expression pattern of two microRNAs (miR-21, miR-29a) and three genes (Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN) at the non-toxic dose which may be called secondary toxicity.

Cytotoxicity of GO (151 ± 11 nm) and GQDs (3.5–8 nm) have been previously evaluated in MCF-7 cells by Tang (Tang et al., 2015) and Bansal (Bansal et al., 2019) and no significant toxicity was observed. Although it has been suggested that size of nanographenes affect their cytotoxicity, we found that GQDs in size of 50 nm and 100 nm of GO sheets were non-toxic after 4 and 24 h in MCF-7 and KMBC/71 cells. Similarly, GO and GQDs were non-toxic for 4 h in HUVEC cells but, these compounds lowered HUVEC cells viability to 85% after 24 h. Generally, HUVEC cells are more sensitive than MCF-7 cells to nanographenes. According to previous reports, GO is about 10 fold more toxic in HUVEC cells compare to other human cell lines (Das et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2010). However, Das et al. concluded that superficial modification on the GO would increase viability of HUVEC cells.

Despite high potential of graphene for medical application, its secondary toxicity in cells is not well-defined. Zhu et al. indicated that exposure of macrophages to low doses of GO (50–300 nm) did not directly induce cytotoxicity in these cells, but increased cadmium (Cd; heavy metal) uptake in macrophage cells and led to increased cell death through apoptosis (Zhu et al., 2018). In another study, it was shown that GQDs (1.5 nm to 4 nm) at non-toxic low concentrations increased the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1b (IL-1b) and IL-8 in macrophage cells. Interestingly they found that high concentrations of GQDs reversed this effect and suppressed the expression of TNF-α, IL-1b and IL-8 (Qin et al., 2014). Furthermore, the effect of GO (40–60 nm) on the expression of miRNAs in non-and toxic doses have been previously reported in two studies conducted on C-elegans (Wu et al., 2014) and GLC-82 cells (Li et al., 2014). They found that GO can affect the signaling pathways controlled by miRNAs. Furthermore, the changes in the expression of miRNAs were demonstrated in several types of nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2014; Alinovi et al., 2017; Li et al., 2011b; Halappanavar et al., 2011; Oh et al., 2015; Eom et al., 2014). A recent study, showed that Silver, Gold and Iron Oxide Nanoparticles in low toxicity doses alter the expression of nine miRNAs in HepG2 Cells (Brzóska et al., 2019).

According to our results, we observed secondary toxicity of GO-100 and GQDs-50 which altered the basal level of miR-21 and miR-29a RNAs in MCF-7, KMBC/71 and HUVEC cells. In previous studies, miR-21 and miR-29a have been suggested as therapeutic targets for breast cancer. MiR-21 targets many important tumor suppressor genes and enhances breast tumor cells proliferation and metastasis. Leucine zipper transcription factor like-1 (LZTFL-1), tropomyosine 1, programed cell death 4 (PDCD4) and maspin are some key tumor suppressor genes which are directly inhibited by high level of miR-21 in breast cancer and led to promotion of epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), tumor invasion and metastasis. On the other hand, elevated level of miR-29a associates with acquisition of resistance to chemotherapy drugs such as adriamycin and docetaxel in breast cancer. It has been suggested that miR-29a exert its role through PTEN/ AKT/GSK3β pathway. In addition to miR-21 and miR-29a, Bax, Bcl2 and PTEN genes also underwent alteration in expression at both mRNA and protein levels. These genes are involved in survival and apoptosis of breast tumor cells. The Bax to Bcl2 protein ratio have been considered as a determinant of cell susceptibility to apoptosis (Jafarinejad-Farsangi et al., 2015). The probability of apoptosis increases when high level of Bax accompanied by low level of Bcl2. Although the non-toxic dose of GO-100 and GQD-50 did not directly induce apoptosis but significant expression changes of Bax and Bcl2 may alter cell susceptibility to apoptosis. PTEN, the other protein with significant increased level in our study, have been previously reported with low expression in breast tumors (Li et al., 2017). Any changes in the expression of PTEN may influence the survival and development of breast tumors.

According to our results, we observed poor and even negative correlation between mRNA and protein expression. There may be some reasons behind it as previously reported (Koussounadis et al., 2015). It may be due to using different time scales for analysis of mRNA and protein expression. Furthermore, regulation of gene expression may occur at post-transcriptional and post-translational levels which affect mRNA and protein stability and half-life (Koussounadis et al., 2015; Velez-Bermudez and Schmidt, 2014; Vogel et al., 2011; Fessler et al., 2002).

Collectively, our results confirmed secondary toxic effect of GO-100 and GQDs-50 at a non-toxic dose on both cellular and molecular level. Higher mitochondrial membrane potential and significant changes in the expression of genes were observed which may cause deviation in the desired outcome of GO and GQDs in medical research. Since, the number of studied genes are limited in this study, whole transcriptome and proteome analysis besides cell viability assays are suggested which can provide valuable insights about molecular changes after treatment with non-toxic doses of graphenes. However, it is necessary to eliminate undesired effects by reducing the exposure time and/or applying chemical changes to GO and GQDs to moderate their secondary toxicity as drug carriers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Kerman University of Medical Sciences (KMU), Iran for financial support (Grant NO: 95000246). The authors acknowledge Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Iran for their assistance. The authors would like to thank Dr. Mariam Shahrokhi for access to KMBC/71 cells (KMU Ethics Committee code: IR.KMU.REC.1398.025).
